Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm 28 and juggling a lot more than just school. I'd carve out intense blocks of time, usually late at night or early mornings on weekends, where I'd really dive deep into the material. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often work through problems physically, writing things out repeatedly or creating diagrams. The 21 hours I put in each week were concentrated and focused, even if they didn't always show up as LMS activity.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be incredibly consistent, studying every evening after work. Other weeks, especially when anxiety hit hard, I'd have to cram everything into a few marathon sessions. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd kick into high gear, but I tried to stay ahead when I could manage my nerves.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check assignment requirements or download materials I could study offline. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't missing anything critical because the fear of falling behind kept me on edge. Honestly, the anxiety of potentially missing a deadline was often what prompted me to check in, even if I didn't stay long.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine because I found it more efficient to download what I needed and work offline. I'd check in periodically, maybe nine times total over the course, but it was always purposeful rather than habitual. My routine centered more around my physical notes and printed materials.
Q5: The downloadable lecture materials and practice problems were lifesavers for me. As a kinesthetic learner, I needed to work through things by hand, so having PDFs I could print and annotate was essential. The videos were useful too, especially the longer ones where concepts were demonstrated step by step. I watched nearly three hours worth because seeing things in action helped cement my understanding.
Q6: I largely ignored the discussion forums beyond my ten posts because they heightened my anxiety. Reading other students' questions sometimes made me doubt whether I understood the material correctly, which sent me spiraling. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings because I had to prioritize what would directly help my exam performance.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam preparation or assignment completion. I needed to see a clear path from the task to my grade because with limited time and high anxiety, I couldn't afford to spend energy on things that felt peripheral. Practical exercises where I could physically work through problems were always the most valuable to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford emotionally or time-wise. Required ones triggered my anxiety because I knew I had to complete them, but that same pressure kept me focused. Optional ones just added to my mental load, making me feel guilty when I skipped them even though I knew I needed to prioritize. The gamified tasks they introduced were interesting but still felt like extra weight.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides, print them out, and cover them in handwritten notes and diagrams. I also used textbooks from the library and would physically work through practice problems on paper. My desk was always covered in notes, flashcards, and highlighted printouts that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for eight or ten hours straight, but all of it was with printed materials or my own handwritten notes. I'd re-work problems from the assignments multiple times on paper to really understand them. The LMS would show nothing during these periods, but that's when I was doing my deepest learning and really mastering the material.
Q11: This was basically my entire approach. I'd log in once, download everything available for a module, and then not return until I needed the next set of materials. I'd study those downloaded files for days or weeks without touching the LMS. It was more efficient for me and reduced the anxiety of constantly being online where I might see things that stressed me out.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt nearly impossible. When I'd see my low attendance percentage or incomplete assignments, I'd feel this wave of fear that made it hard to even open the LMS. During those times, I'd retreat to offline studying because it felt safer and I could control my environment better.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS usage. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing notifications or discussion posts would make things worse. Fatigue from working and studying definitely played a role too. I'd sometimes be too exhausted to engage meaningfully online, so I'd just grab what I needed and retreat to my physical study space.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything because my mind was racing with worry. I'd have videos playing while my thoughts were elsewhere, consumed by fear about whether I was doing enough. Those moments felt hollow, like I was going through the motions without actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they terrified me. The thought of other students reading my contributions and judging them made my anxiety unbearable. I knew participation mattered, but I'd put it off until the last possible moment. Even some assignments got delayed because I was scared my work wouldn't be good enough, despite ultimately scoring well.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd often log in right before something was due to submit work or double-check requirements. The fear of missing a deadline was sometimes paralyzing, but it also forced me into action. My engagement was very much deadline-driven, with activity spiking right before due dates and dropping off in between.
Q17: Feedback was incredibly important to me, though it also triggered anxiety. When I received positive feedback on assignments, it gave me a boost of confidence that helped me engage more. But waiting for feedback was agonizing, and sometimes I'd avoid checking for days because I was scared of what I'd see. The relief of good scores eventually encouraged me to keep pushing.
Q18: The structure felt somewhat overwhelming with 17 online courses to manage. I had to be very strategic about where I put my energy. The gamified tasks they introduced were an interesting change of pace, though they added another layer of things to track. I appreciated clear deadlines and explicit requirements because they helped me prioritize despite my anxiety.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for me as a kinesthetic learner. What made it harder was the expectation of regular online participation and attendance when my anxiety made that difficult. The discussion requirements felt particularly challenging because they required public engagement. Clearer, more structured pathways through the content would have helped me feel less overwhelmed.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, maybe trying to understand what's expected or feeling lost about where to start. They could also be dealing with anxiety like me, where they're checking and rechecking things without being able to commit to actual work. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggle rather than engagement.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was minimal, but I achieved a distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials once and study them extensively on my own. The system only saw my nine logins and low task completion, but it couldn't see the hours I spent with printed notes, working through problems by hand until I truly understood them.
Q22: A course log can accurately show login patterns and submission timestamps, but it completely misses the depth of offline engagement. It can't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or the understanding I built through handwritten practice. It also can't show the emotional experience, the anxiety that kept me away from the platform even while I was studying intensely elsewhere.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until I understand it deeply. For me, that's a physical, kinesthetic process of writing, diagramming, and working through problems repeatedly. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch videos. True engagement is when I can apply concepts confidently, which my exam score reflects even if my LMS activity doesn't.
Q24: The LMS would never show the fear I felt every time I logged in, or the relief when I could retreat to offline studying. It wouldn't capture the late nights working through problems on paper, or how I'd re-read my handwritten notes on the bus. Most importantly, it couldn't show how hard I fought against my anxiety to achieve that distinction, or the real learning that happened far from any screen.
